ccel.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

title: To Anastasius, Bishop.

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Gregory to Anastasius, Bishop of Antioch.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Glory to God in the highest and on earth peace to men of good will (Luke ii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 14), because that great river which once had left the rocks of Antioch dry has returned at length to its proper channel, and waters the subject valleys that are near, so as also to bring forth fruit, some thirty-fold, some sixty-fold, and some an hundred-fold.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For now there is no doubt that many flowers of souls are growing up in its valleys, and that they will come even to ripe fruit through the streams of your tongue.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Wherefore with voice of heart and mouth from our inmost soul we render due praise to Almighty God, and rejoice in your Blessedness, not with you only, but with all who are subject to you.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: I have received the letters of your Holiness, to me most sweet and pleasant, while we ourselves, if I may so speak, are sweating under the same toil with you.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And indeed I know how heavy must be to thee the burden of external cares after those heights of rest, wherein with the hand of the heart thou wert touching heavenly secrets.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But remember that thou rulest an Apostolic See, and assuagest sorrow the more readily from being made all things to all men.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: In the Books of Kings, as your accomplished Holiness knows, a certain man is described who used either hand for the right hand (1 Chron. xii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, with regard to this, I am not doubtful about the lord Anastasius, of old my most sweet and most holy patron, that, while he draws earthly works to heavenly profit, he turns the left hand to the right hand’s use; so that his heavenly intentness may accomplish its work, so to speak, with the right hand, and also, when he is led in his care of temporal things towards the interests of justice, the left hand may acquire the strength of the right.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And indeed these things cannot be without heavy labour and trouble.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But let us remember the labours of those who went before us; and what we endure will not be hard.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For We must through many tribulations enter into the kingdom of God (Acts xiv.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, We were pressed out of measure, yea and above strength, insomuch that we were weary even of life.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But we ourselves, too, had the answer of death in ourselves, that we should not trust in ourselves (2 Cor. i.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And yet The sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the supervening glory which shall be revealed in us (Rom. viii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: How then can we that are weak sheep pass without labour through the heat of this world wherein we know that even rams have suffered under heavy toil?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Further, what tribulations I suffer in this land from the swords of the Lombards, from the iniquities of judges, from the press of business, from the care of subjects, and also from bodily affliction, I am unable to express either by pen or tongue.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Concerning which things even though I might say something briefly, I hesitate, lest to your most holy Charity, while afflicted by your own tribulations, I should add mine also.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But may Almighty God both in the abundance of His loving-kindness fill the mind of your most holy Blessedness with all comfort, and grant at some time, on account of your intercession, to unworthy me to rest from these evils which I suffer.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: These words, as you see, taken from what you had written, I insert in my epistles, that your Blessedness may perceive with regard to Saint Ignatius that he is not only yours, but also ours.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, as we have his master, the Prince of the apostles in common, so also no one of us ought to have to himself alone the disciple of this same Prince.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Moreover, we have received your blessing, which is of sweet smell and of a good savour, with the feelings that were due to it.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And we give thanks to Almighty God that what you do, what you say, and what you give, is fragrant and savoury.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For your life therefore let us say together, let us say all, Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace to men of good will.
